{{title}}:
**Full Trial Title:**  
Beta-blockers Or Placebo for Primary Prophylaxis of oesophageal varices (BOPPP Trial). A blinded, UK multi-centre, clinical effectiveness and cost-effectiveness randomised controlled trial.
{{acronym}}: The trial acronym for the BOPPP trial is "BOPPP".
{{name_of_cheif_investigator}}: Dr. Vishal C. Patel, King's College Hospital NHS Foundation Trust.
{{senior_statistician}}: Dr Ben Carter, King's College London Clinical Trials Unit.
{{description_of_trial}}: The BOPPP trial is a multi-centre, Phase IV, blinded, randomized controlled trial designed to evaluate the clinical effectiveness and cost-effectiveness of carvedilol compared to placebo for the primary prophylaxis of oesophageal varices in patients with cirrhosis and small (grade 1) oesophageal varices. The trial will enroll 740 patients and assess outcomes over a follow-up period of three years, focusing on the rate of all-cause decompensation and associated healthcare costs.
{{investigators}}:
- Dr Vishal C. Patel, King's College Hospital NHS Foundation Trust  
- Dr Mark J. McPhail, King's College Hospital NHS Foundation Trust  
- Dr Ben Carter, King's College London Clinical Trials Unit
{{principle_investigator}}: Dr. Vishal C. Patel, King's College Hospital NHS Foundation Trust.
{{trial_manager}}: Trial Manager: Not explicitly mentioned in the protocol.
{{trial_statisticians}}: Dr Ben Carter, Kings College London Clinical Trials Unit
{{health_economist}}: Dr. Ben Carter, King's College London Clinical Trials Unit.
{{primary_objectives}}:
1. To determine the clinical effectiveness in the reduction of all-cause decompensation in patients treated with carvedilol versus placebo after 3 years.  
2. To determine the cost-effectiveness of Carvedilol in patients with small oesophageal varices.
{{secondary_objectives}}:
To determine additional clinical benefits of Carvedilol versus placebo for: reduction of variceal size progression, need to initiate endoscopic management of varices (endoscopic band ligation (EBL)), deterioration in liver function (assessed by MELD score and Child-Pugh grade) and all-cause mortality.

To investigate how this is best delivered in primary care, by general practice, using qualitative approaches and GP interviews to examine barriers and enablers to implementation.
{{trial_design}}: The BOPPP trial is a multi-centre, Phase IV, parallel-group, blinded (participant, investigator, and analyst) randomized controlled trial designed to evaluate the clinical effectiveness and cost-effectiveness of carvedilol compared to placebo for the primary prophylaxis of oesophageal varices in patients with cirrhosis and small varices. The primary hypothesis is that carvedilol reduces the rate of all-cause decompensation in this patient population over a follow-up period of three years. The trial will assess both clinical outcomes and health economic implications associated with the intervention.
{{duration_of_treatment}}: The duration of treatment for each arm in the BOPPP trial is a total period of 3 years. Participants will receive either oral Carvedilol or placebo, with the trial drug dispensed every 6 months for the duration of the study.
{{follow_up_timepoints}}:
- Baseline visit
- Week 1 visit (+/- 3 days)
- Week 6 safety telephone call (+/- 2 weeks)
- Month 6 visit (+/- 6 weeks)
- Month 12 visit (+/- 6 weeks)
- Month 18 visit (+/- 6 weeks)
- Month 24 visit (+/- 6 weeks)
- Month 30 visit (+/- 6 weeks)
- Month 36 visit (+/- 6 weeks)
- At variceal bleed
- At trial completion (via registry)
{{visit_windows}}:
The visit windows for assessments in the BOPPP trial are as follows:

- **Pre-Screening**: All patients with cirrhosis and small oesophageal varices identified at surveillance endoscopy will be screened for inclusion in this trial.
- **Eligibility/Screening Visit**: Confirm eligibility and photo-document gastroscopy.
- **Informed Consent**: Patients who meet the inclusion and exclusion criteria will be offered the opportunity to participate in the trial.
- **Baseline Visit**: Randomisation will occur at this visit, along with the collection of baseline data.
- **Week 1 Visit (+/- 3 days)**: Participants will be reviewed for adherence and any adverse events related to the IMP.
- **Week 6 Visit (+/- 2 weeks)**: Safety telephone review to assess for any adverse events related to the IMP.
- **Month 6 Visit (+/- 6 weeks)**: Clinical review, laboratory testing, and completion of questionnaires.
- **Month 12 Visit (+/- 6 weeks)**: Clinical review, laboratory testing, surveillance liver ultrasound, and varices surveillance gastroscopy.
- **Month 18 Visit (+/- 6 weeks)**: Clinical review, laboratory testing, and completion of questionnaires.
- **Month 24 Visit (+/- 6 weeks)**: Clinical review, laboratory testing, surveillance liver ultrasound, and varices surveillance gastroscopy.
- **Month 30 Visit (+/- 6 weeks)**: Clinical review, laboratory testing, and completion of questionnaires.
- **Month 36 Visit (+/- 6 weeks)**: Clinical review, laboratory testing, surveillance liver ultrasound, and varices surveillance gastroscopy.
- **At Variceal Bleed**: Assessments will be conducted as per the defined protocol.
- **At Trial Completion**: Final assessments will be conducted via registry.
{{data_collection_procedures}}: Data collection for the BOPPP trial will be conducted using a combination of electronic case report forms (eCRF) and source data worksheets, with data entered into the Infer Med MACRO database. Key sources of data will include clinical assessments, laboratory test results, and patient-reported outcomes collected at various time points: baseline, week 1, and at 6-month intervals up to 36 months. Specific assessments will include vital signs, liver function tests, Child Pugh and MELD scores, quality of life using the EQ-5D-5L, and healthcare usage questionnaires. Additionally, adverse events will be recorded throughout the trial, with data discrepancies flagged for resolution. All data will be stored securely and processed in accordance with relevant legal and regulatory requirements.
{{inclusion_criteria}}:
- Age 18 years and over  
- Cirrhosis and portal hypertension, defined by any 2 of the following:  
  - Characteristic clinical examination findings; one or more of  
  - Characteristic liver function tests  
  - Haematological panel  
  - Coagulation profile abnormalities  
  - Characteristic radiological findings; one or more of  
    - Heterogeneous liver with irregular contour  
    - Splenomegaly  
    - Ascites  
    - Varices  
    - Recanalized umbilical vein  
    - FibroScan liver stiffness measurement >15 kPa without other explanation  
    - Fibrosis score > ISHAK stage 4 on liver biopsy (presence of a relevant fibrosis score by biopsy does not require additional clinical examination/radiological/FibroScan supporting evidence)  
- Small oesophageal varices diagnosed within the last 6 months, defined as ≤5 mm in diameter or varices which completely disappear on moderate insufflation at gastroscopy.  
- Not received a beta-blocker in the last week  
- Capacity to provide informed consent
{{exclusion_criteria}}:
- Non-cirrhotic portal hypertension  
- Current medium/large oesophageal varices (defined as >5 mm in diameter)  
- Previous medium/large oesophageal varices (defined as >5 mm in diameter), which decreased in size with curative therapy  
- Gastric (IGV and GOV2), duodenal, rectal or other ectopic varices with or without evidence of recent bleeding. For gastric varices, this includes:  
  1. IGV-1 and IGV-2 (isolated gastric varices)  
  2. GOV2 (gastric varices continuing into the cardia)  
  3. Note GOV1 (gastric varices continuing into the lesser curve) are not an exclusion if present with small oesophageal varices  
- Previous variceal haemorrhage  
- Previous band ligation or glue injection of oesophageal and/or gastric varices  
- Red signs accompanying varices at endoscopy  
- Known intolerance to beta blockers  
- Contraindications to beta blocker use:  
  - Heart rate <50 bpm  
  - Known 2nd degree or higher heart block  
  - Sick sinus syndrome  
  - Systolic blood pressure <85 mmHg  
  - Chronic airways obstruction (asthma/COPD)  
  - Floppy Iris Syndrome  
  - CYP2D6 Poor Metaboliser  
  - History of cardiogenic shock  
  - History of severe hypersensitivity reaction to beta-blockers  
  - Untreated phaeochromocytoma  
  - Severe peripheral vascular disease  
  - Prinzmetal angina  
  - NYHA IV heart failure  
- Unable to provide informed consent  
- Child Pugh C cirrhosis  
- Already receiving a beta-blocker for another reason that cannot be discontinued  
- Graft cirrhosis post liver transplantation  
- Evidence of active malignancy without curative therapy planned  
- Pregnant or lactating women  
- Women of child bearing potential not willing to use adequate contraception during the period of IMP dosing  
- Patients who have been on a CTIMP within the previous 3 months  
- Clinical symptoms consistent with COVID-19 (a high temperature, a new continuous cough or a loss or change to sense of smell or taste) at the time of randomisation
{{primary_outcome_measures}}:
**Primary Outcomes:**

- **Time to first decompensating event:** The duration from randomization to the occurrence of the first decompensating event, measured in months over a follow-up period of 3 years.

- **Cost-utility of NSBB:** The economic evaluation of the cost-effectiveness of non-selective beta-blockers (NSBB) compared to placebo, assessed over the trial follow-up period of 3 years, measured in terms of cost per quality-adjusted life year (QALY).
{{secondary_outcome_measures}}:
- **Estimation of the 1 and 3-year variceal bleed rate by allocation**: This outcome measures the incidence of variceal bleeding events at 1 and 3 years post-baseline, expressed as a rate per 100 patient-years.

- **Progression to medium/large varices requiring clinical intervention**: This outcome assesses the occurrence of patients progressing to medium or large oesophageal varices that necessitate clinical intervention, evaluated at 3 years post-baseline.

- **Development of gastric, duodenal, or ectopic varices**: This outcome measures the incidence of new gastric, duodenal, or ectopic varices detected at gastroscopy, assessed at 3 years post-baseline.

- **Composite of progression in variceal size or variceal bleeding**: This outcome combines the events of variceal size progression and variceal bleeding, evaluated at 3 years post-baseline.

- **Quality of life, EQ-5D-5L**: This outcome measures the quality of life using the EQ-5D-5L questionnaire, assessed at baseline and at 6, 12, 18, 24, 30, and 36 months.
{{mediator_of_treatment}}: - **Quality of Life (QoL)**: Assessed using the EQ-5D-5L questionnaire at baseline and at 6-month intervals throughout the trial.
{{moderator_of_treatment}}:
- **Patient Age**: Assessed through demographic data collection at baseline.
- **Gender**: Assessed through demographic data collection at baseline.
- **Disease Severity**: Assessed using prognostic scoring systems (Child Pugh, MELD, UKELD, CLIF-C AD) calculated at baseline and 6-monthly visits.
{{process_indicators}}:
- **Fidelity**: Adherence to the trial protocol, including the correct administration of the investigational medicinal product (IMP) and adherence to scheduled visits.
- **Exposure**: Duration and dosage of carvedilol or placebo received by participants, including any dose modifications or interruptions.
- **Recruitment Rate**: Number of participants enrolled in the trial relative to the target sample size of 740 patients.
- **Retention Rate**: Proportion of participants who complete the trial as planned, including follow-up visits and data collection.
- **Adverse Events Reporting**: Frequency and nature of adverse events (AEs) and serious adverse events (SAEs) reported during the trial.
- **Protocol Deviations**: Instances of deviations from the trial protocol, including reasons for deviations and their impact on data integrity.
- **Quality of Life Assessments**: Completion rates of the EQ-5D-5L quality of life questionnaire at scheduled intervals. 
- **Healthcare Resource Utilization**: Collection of data on healthcare usage through participant questionnaires at specified time points.
{{adverse_events}}:
**Adverse Event Reporting Summary**

Adverse events (AEs) will be monitored throughout the BOPPP trial, defined as any untoward medical occurrence in a participant who has received the investigational medicinal product (IMP), irrespective of whether it is related to the IMP. Adverse reactions (ARs) are defined as any untoward and unintended response to the IMP that is related to any dose administered. Serious adverse events (SAEs) are defined as AEs that result in death, are life-threatening, require hospitalization or prolongation of existing hospitalization, result in persistent or significant disability or incapacity, consist of a congenital anomaly or birth defect, or require intervention to prevent permanent impairment or damage. Unexpected serious adverse reactions (SUSARs) are those that are not consistent with the information set out in the summary of product characteristics (SmPC) for the IMP.

All AEs, ARs, and SAEs will be reported immediately (within 24 hours) by the site Principal Investigator (PI) or delegate to the King’s Health Partners Clinical Trials Office (KHP-CTO) and the Chief Investigator (CI) for review. The reporting will adhere to the Medicines for Human Use (Clinical Trials) Regulations 2004 and its amendments. AEs will be graded according to the Common Terminology Criteria for Adverse Events (CTCAE) version 5.0, which classifies AEs from Grade 1 (mild) to Grade 5 (death related to AE).

The coding system for AEs will utilize the Medical Dictionary for Regulatory Activities (MedDRA) to ensure standardized terminology. Summaries of AEs, ARs, SAEs, and SUSARs will be presented to the Data Monitoring Committee (DMC) at regular intervals, including the frequency and severity of events, and will be stratified by treatment allocation. The trial will also report on the incidence of AEs in relation to the overall number of participants and the duration of exposure to the IMP.
{{only_baseline_measures}}:
- Demographics
- Medical History
- Targeted physical exam
- Weight / Height
- Vital signs (Blood Pressure/Heart Rate)
- Full Blood Count (FBC)
- International Normalised Ratio (INR)
- Liver Profile
- Renal and Bone Profile
- Disease severity and prognostic scores (Child Pugh, MELD, UKELD, CLIF-C AD)
- AUDIT-C/alcohol questionnaire
{{additional_follow_up_measures}}:
- Quality of Life assessment using the EQ-5D-5L questionnaire at baseline, 6, 12, 18, 24, 30, and 36 months.
- Healthcare resource usage assessment at baseline, 6, 12, 18, 24, 30, and 36 months.
- AUDIT-C questionnaire for alcohol consumption at baseline, 6, 12, 18, 24, 30, and 36 months.
- Clinical review including medical history, physical exam, and vital signs at Month 6, 12, 18, 24, 30, and 36.
- Blood tests (FBC, INR, liver profile, renal and bone profile) at Month 6, 12, 18, 24, 30, and 36.
- Surveillance liver ultrasound for hepatocellular carcinoma at Month 6, 12, 18, 24, 30, and 36.
- Variceal haemorrhage status assessment at each visit (Month 6, 12, 18, 24, 30, and 36).
- Assessment of progression in Child Pugh score, MELD score, UKELD score, and CLIF-C AD score at each visit (Month 6, 12, 18, 24, 30, and 36).
{{screening_recruitment_consort}}:
- **Screened**: Approximately 4,000 patients with cirrhosis assessed for eligibility following detection of small oesophageal varices at gastroscopy.
- **Excluded**: 3,260 patients not meeting inclusion criteria, refused to participate, or for other reasons.
- **Eligible**: 740 patients meeting the inclusion criteria.
- **Randomised**: 740 patients randomly assigned to either the carvedilol (active arm) or placebo (control arm).
- **Allocated**: 370 patients allocated to the carvedilol group and 370 patients allocated to the placebo group.
- **Treated**: All allocated patients received their respective treatment (carvedilol or placebo).
- **Followed-up**: Patients followed up at scheduled visits (Month 6, Month 12, Month 18, Month 24, Month 30, Month 36) for clinical assessments and data collection.
- **Analysed**: Data from all patients will be analysed according to the intention to treat (ITT) principle.
{{treatment_compliance_definitition}}: Treatment compliance/adherence in the BOPPP trial will be assessed by means of a pill count undertaken by the research nurse or practitioner at each protocolled research visit. Participants will be recorded as having complied with taking the trial medication if at least 50% of the number of pills that should have been removed were removed; that is, less than 50% of the trial IMP tablets have been returned. Situations where patients have returned more than 50% of the trial IMP must be recorded as a protocol deviation.
{{adherence_to_treatment}}: Adherence to the allocated treatment will be assessed through pill counts conducted by the research nurse or practitioner at each scheduled visit, including the Week 1 visit, Month 6, and subsequent 6-monthly follow-ups until Month 36. Participants will be recorded as compliant if at least 50% of the trial investigational medicinal product (IMP) tablets that should have been removed were returned. Any instances where participants return more than 50% of the trial IMP will be documented as protocol deviations. The adherence data will be summarised in the final analysis, detailing the proportion of participants who adhered to the treatment regimen across both arms of the trial.
{{descriptive_statistics}}: Descriptive statistics for outcome measures will include means, standard deviations, medians, interquartile ranges, and frequencies for continuous and categorical variables, respectively. These summaries will be calculated for each treatment group (Carvedilol and Placebo) at baseline, and at each follow-up time point (Week 1, Month 6, Month 12, Month 18, Month 24, Month 30, and Month 36). Additionally, the analysis will include the estimation of the 1- and 3-year variceal bleed rates by allocation, along with the associated number needed to treat, and will be reported until the 3-year follow-up.
{{descriptive_of_intervention}}: Descriptive summaries will include the dosing regimen for the investigational medicinal product (IMP), Carvedilol, which will start at 6.25 mg once daily (OD) and may be titrated up to a maximum of 12.5 mg OD based on patient tolerance and clinical response. The summaries will detail the timing of dose titration visits, specifically at week 1 and week 6 post-randomisation, where research nurses or practitioners will assess adherence, adverse events, and vital signs to determine the appropriateness of dose adjustments. Additionally, the role of the trial personnel, including research nurses and practitioners responsible for monitoring patient safety and managing dose titration, will be outlined to provide context on the delivery of the intervention.
{{descriptive_concomitant_medications}}: Concomitant medication data will be collected at each trial visit, including baseline, week 1, and subsequent 6-monthly follow-ups until the end of the trial. The data will encompass all medications taken by participants during the trial, with a focus on those that may interact with the investigational medicinal product (Carvedilol). Summary statistics will be presented as counts and percentages for categorical variables, and means with standard deviations for continuous variables, stratified by treatment allocation (Carvedilol vs. placebo). This summary will provide insights into the medication usage patterns and potential interactions within the study population.
{{visit_window_deviation}}: Visit-window deviations will be classified based on the timing of the visit relative to the scheduled visit window. If a participant's visit occurs outside of the specified ±6 weeks window but within a 12-week window, the outcome data can still be utilized for that visit. However, if a visit occurs beyond the 12-week window, it will be considered a protocol deviation and documented accordingly. All deviations will be reported to ensure compliance with the trial protocol and maintain the integrity of the data collected.
{{primary_estimand}}:
**Primary Estimand**

The primary estimand for the BOPPP trial is defined as follows:

- **Population**: The population of interest includes all patients with cirrhosis and small oesophageal varices who are randomized to receive either carvedilol or placebo, as per the intention-to-treat (ITT) principle.

- **Endpoint**: The primary endpoint is the time to first decompensating event, which encompasses any occurrence of all-cause decompensation as defined in the protocol, including variceal hemorrhage, new or worsening ascites, hepatic encephalopathy, spontaneous bacterial peritonitis, hepatorenal syndrome, increase in Child Pugh Grade by 1 or MELD by 5 points, and liver-related mortality.

- **Treatment Condition**: The treatment condition refers to the administration of carvedilol at a dose of 6.25 mg to 12.5 mg once daily compared to placebo.

- **Intercurrent Events Handling Strategy**: Intercurrent events, such as the occurrence of a decompensating event, will be handled by classifying patients who experience any of the defined decompensation events as having treatment failure. Patients who do not experience any decompensation during the follow-up period will be censored at their last time of follow-up. Additionally, participants who progress to medium/large varices will have their treatment discontinued, and they may be unblinded if it influences their subsequent clinical management.

- **Population-Level Summary**: The primary estimand will be summarized at the population level by reporting the adjusted hazard ratio for time to first decompensating event between the carvedilol and placebo groups, along with the associated 95% confidence interval and p-value, using a Cox proportional hazards model adjusted for patient age, gender, and disease severity.
{{confidence_interval_p_value}}: The nominal significance level for the BOPPP trial will be set at 0.05, and confidence intervals for reporting treatment effects will be presented at the 95% confidence level.
{{primary_analysis_model}}: The primary outcome, time to first decompensating event, will be analyzed using a Cox proportional hazards model. This model will be adjusted for covariates including patient age, gender, and disease severity, as indicated by baseline prognostic scores such as Child Pugh and MELD. The treatment effect will be parameterized as the hazard ratio comparing the carvedilol group to the placebo group, with site included as a shared frailty term to account for potential clustering of outcomes within sites. The assumption of proportional hazards will be visually assessed using Kaplan-Meier plots and log-log plots, and the results will be reported alongside the adjusted hazard ratio, 95% confidence interval, and p-value.
{{intercurrent_events_and_analysis}}:
**Anticipated Intercurrent Events and Corresponding Strategies:**

- **Variceal Haemorrhage:**
  - Stop IMP permanently.
  - Start NSBB as per standard of care.

- **Progression to Medium/Large Varices:**
  - Stop IMP permanently.
  - Unblinding may occur if it influences clinical management.

- **New or Worsening Ascites:**
  - Continue IMP unless hospitalized.
  - Assess at next visit.

- **New or Worsening Hepatic Encephalopathy:**
  - Continue IMP unless hospitalized.
  - Assess at next visit.

- **Increase in Child Pugh Grade by 1 or MELD by 5:**
  - Continue IMP unless hospitalized.
  - Assess at next visit.

- **Spontaneous Bacterial Peritonitis:**
  - Pause IMP while hospitalized.
  - Resume when stable.

- **Hepatorenal Syndrome:**
  - Pause IMP while hospitalized.
  - Resume when stable.

- **Liver-Related Mortality:**
  - Not applicable for IMP or trial continuation.

**Supplementary Analyses:**
- Collect data on all intercurrent events for endpoint analysis.
- Monitor and report protocol deviations related to intercurrent events.
- Ensure adherence to follow-up for participants experiencing intercurrent events.
{{secondary_estimands}}:
**Estimands for Secondary Outcomes**

The estimands for secondary outcomes in the BOPPP trial will differ from the primary estimand, which focuses on the time to first decompensating event. For secondary outcomes, the estimands will encompass various clinical events and quality of life measures over the follow-up period. Specifically, the estimands for secondary outcomes include:

1. **Variceal Bleed Rate**: The estimand will assess the incidence of variceal bleeding at 1 and 3 years, comparing the rates between the carvedilol and placebo groups. This will be measured as the number of events per group, allowing for a direct comparison of treatment effects.

2. **Progression to Medium/Large Varices**: The estimand will focus on the proportion of participants progressing to medium or large varices requiring clinical intervention, evaluated at the 3-year mark. This will provide insight into the effectiveness of carvedilol in preventing variceal progression.

3. **Development of Gastric, Duodenal, or Ectopic Varices**: The estimand will capture the occurrence of new varices in the gastrointestinal tract, assessed at the 3-year follow-up, to evaluate the broader impact of carvedilol on variceal development.

4. **Composite Outcomes**: The estimand for the composite of progression in variceal size or variceal bleeding will be defined as the occurrence of either event by 3 years, providing a comprehensive measure of treatment efficacy.

5. **Quality of Life (QoL)**: The estimand for QoL will be based on the EQ-5D-5L scores collected at baseline and at 6-month intervals up to 3 years. This will assess the impact of treatment on participants' health-related quality of life over time.

These estimands will be analyzed using appropriate statistical methods, accounting for the intention-to-treat population, and will provide a comprehensive understanding of the treatment effects of carvedilol beyond the primary endpoint of all-cause decompensation.
{{secondary_analysis}}:
**Statistical Analysis Plan: Secondary Outcomes Analysis Approach**

Secondary outcomes will be analyzed using appropriate statistical methods tailored to the nature of the data collected. Continuous data will be assessed using a general linear mixed effects model, incorporating random effects for both patient and site, while adjusting for fixed effects of patient age and disease severity. The mean differences between the allocation groups will be reported alongside 95% confidence intervals (CIs) and p-values to evaluate the significance of the findings.

For dichotomous outcomes, a mixed effects logistic regression model will be employed, similarly accounting for random effects at the patient and site levels, and adjusting for the same fixed effects as in the continuous data analysis. This approach will ensure that the variability across different sites and individual patient characteristics is appropriately considered, providing robust estimates of treatment effects for the secondary endpoints. All analyses will be conducted on an intention-to-treat basis, ensuring that all randomized participants are included in their allocated groups regardless of adherence or protocol deviations.
{{time_points}}: The analysis time points for outcomes in the BOPPP trial will be conducted at baseline, and at 6, 12, 18, 24, 30, and 36 months, with a permissible window of +/- 6 weeks for each scheduled visit. If a patient presents with a variceal bleed between assessments, the outcomes will be reported on the date of presentation. For visits occurring outside the 6-week window but within a 12-week window, the outcome data can still be utilized for that visit. All relevant data collected during these time points will be used to assess the primary and secondary endpoints as outlined in the protocol.
{{Stratification_and_clustering}}: In the analysis models for the BOPPP trial, stratification factors such as patient age, gender, and disease severity will be accounted for by including these variables as covariates in the Cox proportional hazards model used to analyze the primary outcome of time to all-cause decompensation. Additionally, site will be fitted as a shared frailty term within the model to account for clustering effects due to the multi-centre design of the trial, ensuring that the analysis appropriately adjusts for potential correlations among patients treated at the same site.
{{missing_items_in_scales}}: The protocol does not explicitly outline the handling of missing items within multi-item scales or subscales, including any prorating rules. Therefore, the approach to managing missing data for these scales will be determined in accordance with standard practices in statistical analysis, which may include methods such as imputation or exclusion based on the extent and nature of the missing data. Further details on specific handling procedures will be defined in the Statistical Analysis Plan (SAP) prior to the analysis.
{{missing_baseline_data}}: Missing baseline covariate data will be handled using multiple imputation techniques to ensure that the analyses retain the maximum amount of information while minimizing bias. The imputation will be performed prior to the main statistical analyses, and the results will be pooled across the multiple datasets generated to provide robust estimates. Sensitivity analyses will also be conducted to assess the impact of missing data on the primary and secondary outcomes, ensuring that the conclusions drawn from the trial are valid and reliable.
{{missing_data_sensitivity_analysis}}: In the BOPPP trial, sensitivity analyses for missing outcome data will be conducted using Multiple Imputation to address potential biases arising from missing data. This approach will allow for the estimation of missing values based on observed data, thereby preserving the integrity of the dataset and ensuring that the analyses remain robust. The impact of missing data on the primary and secondary outcomes will be assessed, and the results will be compared to those obtained from the complete case analysis to evaluate the robustness of the findings. Additionally, alternative methods for handling missing data, such as Last Observation Carried Forward (LOCF) and sensitivity analyses for Missing Not at Random (MNAR) scenarios, may be explored to further assess the reliability of the trial results.
{{multiple_comparisons}}: In the BOPPP trial, no formal adjustment for multiplicity will be made as the primary endpoint is clearly defined as the time to first decompensating event, and the secondary endpoints are exploratory in nature. The analysis will focus on the primary outcome, and while secondary outcomes will be reported, they will be interpreted with caution due to the potential for multiplicity effects. The primary analysis will be conducted using an intention-to-treat approach, ensuring that all randomized participants are included in the analysis regardless of adherence to the treatment protocol.
{{analysis_for_noncompliance}}: In the BOPPP trial, non-compliance will be addressed through the analysis of both intention-to-treat (ITT) and per-protocol populations. The ITT population will include all randomized participants and will be used to assess the primary outcome of all-cause decompensation, regardless of adherence to the intervention. Participants who experience any of the all-cause decompensation events will be classified as having treatment failure and recorded as an event. The per-protocol population will exclude participants who violate the study protocol, such as those who progress in variceal size but do not experience any type of decompensation, and will be censored in the analysis of the primary outcome. This dual approach allows for a comprehensive evaluation of the treatment effect while accounting for variations in compliance.
{{model_assumption_checks}}: In the BOPPP trial, the primary outcome, time to all-cause decompensation, will be analyzed using a Cox proportional hazards model. To ensure the validity of this model, the assumption of proportional hazards will be visually assessed using Kaplan-Meier plots and log-log plots, accompanied by an at-risk table for the two allocation groups. If the proportionality assumption is found to be violated, alternative modeling strategies, such as stratification by covariates or the use of time-varying covariates, will be considered to appropriately address the violation and ensure robust analysis of the trial data.
{{other_sensitivity_analysis}}: In addition to addressing missing data through multiple imputation, sensitivity analyses will be conducted to assess the robustness of the primary outcome results against variations in the assumptions made during the analysis. Specifically, alternative parametric specifications will be tested to evaluate the impact of different modeling approaches on the estimated treatment effects. This will help to determine if the conclusions drawn from the primary analysis remain consistent under varying assumptions, thereby enhancing the reliability of the findings regarding the effectiveness of carvedilol versus placebo in reducing all-cause decompensation in patients with small oesophageal varices.
{{subgroup_analysis}}: No subgroup analysis will be conducted.
{{any_additional_exploratory_analysis}}: No additional analysis will be conducted.
{{interim_analysis}}: An internal pilot is planned for the BOPPP trial. The objectives of the internal pilot are to determine the feasibility of the trial by assessing recruitment, retention, and acceptability. This pilot will be conducted 12 months after recruitment opens. The stopping rules for the internal pilot include the requirement to have opened at least 8 sites with at least one patient randomised at each site, randomising at least 80 patients, and achieving a retention rate of at least 70%. If these criteria are met, the trial will progress without any alpha/sample-size adjustments. No interim analysis for efficacy or harm is planned beyond this feasibility assessment.
